moveAnnotations.annotation: We're playing the Grob Opening, an unusual but principled move that immediately signals we're looking for something unconventional. By advancing the g-pawn, we prepare to fianchetto our bishop on g2, where it will control the long diagonal (a1-h8) and exert pressure on d5—a key square we'll want to dispute. This move is risky because it weakens our kingside, so we need to develop quickly and create concrete threats before Black can exploit our exposed king. The Grob works best when you understand it's a fighting weapon that demands active play from move one.

Black naturally strikes in the center, claiming space and establishing a presence on d4. This is the most common response and gives Black a solid pawn duo that controls key squares. Black's strategy here is straightforward: occupy the center while we reorganize our somewhat irregular setup.

We complete our fianchetto, placing the bishop on its ideal long diagonal. The Bg2 now monitors d5 (putting pressure on Black's pawn majority) and supports our future piece play. This is the natural follow-up to g4, creating a coherent setup rather than just moving pawns randomly—the bishop becomes our anchor piece controlling critical squares from g2.

Black advances confidently in the center, challenging our fianchettoed bishop and staking a claim to space. This move opens lines for Black's queen and bishop, creating a dynamic position. By pushing d5, Black is essentially saying "I won't let you control the center"—it's an aggressive response to our unconventional setup.

This is the key tactical thrust of the Hurst Attack! We strike at Black's advanced d5 pawn, forcing a decision. By playing c4, we challenge the pawn directly and prepare to either capture on d5 or support our pieces in the center depending on Black's response. This move demonstrates the real point of our system: instead of playing passively after g4, we counterattack in the center immediately, creating tactical complications that reward our willingness to play dynamically. The position is now sharp and concrete—exactly what the Grob player wants.
